title: Ezekiel 30
subtitle: The Day of the LORD Against Egypt

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Oracle Against Egypt

sections.header: Verses 1-9 — Wail, for the Day Is Near

heb.paragraph: The yom YHWH ('Day of the LORD') is one of the most significant eschatological concepts in prophetic literature. Originally associated with Israel's expectation of God fighting for them (cf. Amos 5:18-20), the prophets transformed it into a day of universal judgment. Ezekiel applies this concept to Egypt, extending YHWH's jurisdiction beyond Israel to all nations. The day brings 'clouds'—a theophanic element associated with divine presence in judgment (cf. Joel 2:2; Zeph 1:15).

The phrase 'a time of doom for the nations' (et goyim, v. 3) uses the pregnant word ʿet, meaning an appointed time—a kairos moment of divine intervention. This implies that Egypt's judgment is not arbitrary but occurs at God's determined time, reinforcing the theme of divine sovereignty over historical processes.

refs.note: Amos first reversed Israel's expectations about the Day of the LORD: it would be darkness, not light. Ezekiel extends this reversal to Egypt, showing that no nation is exempt from divine reckoning.
Joel describes the Day of the LORD with identical imagery: clouds, darkness, and doom. The shared vocabulary suggests a common prophetic tradition about God's ultimate judgment.
Zephaniah's sweeping Day-of-the-LORD oracle against all creation provides the broadest parallel. Both prophets use the concept to assert YHWH's universal kingship.

notes.note: The 'Day of the LORD' is a technical prophetic term for any time God intervenes in judgment. Here it applies to Egypt, but it ultimately points toward the final eschatological Day when all nations face God's reckoning.
A sword comes against Egypt, and anguish grips Cush (modern Sudan/Ethiopia). Egypt's African allies are drawn into the catastrophe.
The allies listed—Cush, Put, Lud, Arabia, Libya—represent the full extent of Egypt's mercenary forces and vassal states. All will fall with Egypt.
Messengers in ships will terrify complacent Cush. The news of Egypt's fall will produce panic among nations that assumed Egypt was invincible.

Calvin draws attention to the universal scope of the Day of the LORD: though applied to Egypt, it anticipates the final judgment of all nations. God's justice knows no ethnic or geographic boundaries.
Calvin notes that the allies who trusted in Egypt will share its destruction—a perpetual warning against placing confidence in human alliances rather than in God.

The Hebrew yom le-YHWH (literally 'a day belonging to the LORD') indicates a time when God asserts his sovereign authority through decisive action. The 'day of clouds' (yom ʿanan) echoes theophanic storm imagery.
The identity of 'the people of the covenant land' (bene erets habberit) is debated. It may refer to Jewish mercenaries in Egypt, allied peoples under treaty obligation, or inhabitants of Canaan.

Block identifies this as an independent oracle using Day-of-the-LORD traditions, without a date formula—suggesting it may be a later theological reflection rather than a specific historical pronouncement. The catalog of nations (vv. 5-6) is structured as concentric circles of judgment radiating outward from Egypt. Block highlights the irony of Cush being terrified: they are geographically beyond Egypt yet cannot escape YHWH's reach.

Zimmerli classifies this as a 'doom oracle' (Untergangsspruch) distinct from the other Egypt oracles in its eschatological register. The Day-of-the-LORD framework elevates the judgment from historical to cosmic significance. Zimmerli argues this oracle draws on pre-exilic prophetic traditions (Amos, Zephaniah) and applies them to the new context of Egypt's impending fall.

hist.context: This undated oracle expands the Egypt judgment theme into cosmic proportions using Day-of-the-LORD language. The list of nations falling alongside Egypt (vv. 5-6)—Cush, Put, Lud, Arabia, Libya, and 'the people of the covenant land'—represents Egypt's entire sphere of influence. The 'people of the covenant land' (v. 5) likely refers to Jewish mercenaries stationed at Elephantine and other Egyptian garrisons, whose fate was bound to Egypt's.

sections.header: Verses 10-19 — The Destruction of Egypt's Cities

heb.paragraph: The shephatim YHWH executes against Egypt echo the 'judgments' God inflicted on Egypt during the Exodus (cf. Exod 7:4; 12:12). Ezekiel thus frames the Babylonian conquest as a second Exodus in reverse—this time God's judgments come not to liberate Israel from Egypt but to punish Egypt itself. The theological continuity between the original Exodus plagues and this prophesied devastation reinforces YHWH's consistent character as judge of imperial arrogance.

refs.note: God declared at the first Exodus: 'I will bring judgment on all the gods of Egypt.' Ezekiel's systematic destruction of Egypt's major cult cities fulfills the same theological purpose against Egypt's religious infrastructure.
Jeremiah prophesied from Tahpanhes that Nebuchadnezzar would set his throne over Egypt. Ezekiel and Jeremiah thus corroborate each other's announcements of Egypt's fall from two different locations.

notes.note: Nebuchadnezzar is identified as the human agent of divine judgment. God uses pagan rulers as instruments of his will, a theme seen throughout prophetic literature.

Memphis (Noph), Egypt's ancient capital, will lose its idols and princes. The emphasis on ending idolatry connects this oracle to the theological purpose of judgment: exposing false gods as powerless.

Thebes (No, or No-Amon), the great cult center of Amun in Upper Egypt, will be set on fire. Pelusium (Sin), the eastern frontier fortress, will writhe in agony. The catalog moves from religious to military targets.
Tahpanhes, where Jewish refugees fled (Jer 43:7), will see its day darkened. The irony is sharp: the place Judeans chose for safety becomes a site of judgment.

Calvin sees the destruction of Memphis's idols as God's jealousy in action: YHWH will not share his glory with carved images. The fall of Egypt's religious infrastructure proves that its gods were always nothing.
Calvin notes God's patient specificity—naming each city individually rather than pronouncing generic doom. This demonstrates that God's judgments are precise, not random destruction.

Noph (Hebrew nop) is Memphis, located south of modern Cairo. The reference to 'no longer a prince in Egypt' may indicate the end of native Egyptian rule, fulfilled when Persian, Greek, and Roman rulers governed Egypt.
Pathros is Upper Egypt. No (Thebes) was the great religious capital dedicated to Amun. Its destruction would be as devastating to Egyptian identity as Jerusalem's fall was to Judah.

Block notes the rhetorical structure: Nebuchadnezzar is introduced (v. 10), then each city is named with its specific fate. The geographic sweep from delta to Upper Egypt demonstrates the comprehensiveness of divine judgment. Block highlights the wordplay between Sin (Pelusium, 'fortress') and the verb 'writhe' (khul)—the fortress will convulse in labor pains rather than standing firm.

Zimmerli identifies this city catalog as drawing on Egyptian geographic knowledge available in the Neo-Babylonian period. The selection of cult centers (Memphis, Thebes, Heliopolis) alongside military strongholds (Pelusium, Tahpanhes) suggests Ezekiel targets both Egypt's religious ideology and its military confidence. Zimmerli views this as systematically dismantling every pillar of Egyptian self-assurance.

hist.context: Ezekiel catalogs Egyptian cities that will fall to Nebuchadnezzar: Memphis (Noph), Thebes (No), Pelusium (Sin), Heliopolis (On), Bubastis (Pi-beseth), and Tahpanhes. This geographic tour covers Upper and Lower Egypt, major cult centers, and military strongholds. Memphis was the administrative capital; Thebes was the great religious center of Amun; Heliopolis was the center of sun worship; Pelusium guarded the eastern delta approach. No corner of Egypt's civilization is spared.

heb.paragraph: The zeroʿa (arm) of Pharaoh is a political metaphor for military power and royal authority. YHWH has already broken one arm (v. 21, likely referring to Hophra's failed attempt to relieve the siege of Jerusalem in 588 BC), and will break the other as well. Meanwhile, YHWH 'strengthens the arms of the king of Babylon' (v. 24)—the same body-part metaphor applied inversely. The image of Pharaoh dropping his sword with a groan captures total military impotence.

refs.note: Pharaoh's army marched north to relieve Jerusalem but retreated when the Babylonians turned to engage them. This is the 'broken arm' of Ezekiel 30:21—a military failure interpreted as divine judgment.
Daniel's vision of empires includes armies being 'swept away' and broken, echoing Ezekiel's arm-breaking imagery applied to successive empires.

notes.note: Dated April 587 BC, shortly after Pharaoh Hophra's abortive attempt to relieve Jerusalem. The 'broken arm' refers to this military failure. The arm was not bound up or healed—Egypt had no recovery.
God will break both arms of Pharaoh—the broken one and the strong one—leaving him completely powerless. The sword will fall from his hand, symbolizing total military collapse.
In stark contrast, God strengthens the arms of Babylon's king. The imagery presents YHWH as the ultimate power behind international affairs, arming one king while disarming another.
Egypt's scattering among nations mirrors Israel's exile. The recognition formula ('they will know that I am the LORD') closes the oracle, emphasizing the revelatory purpose of judgment.

Calvin notes the bitter irony: God breaks Egypt's arms while strengthening Babylon's. This should humble all earthly powers, for their strength depends entirely on God's will. No army prevails except by divine permission.

Calvin emphasizes the scattering of Egypt among nations as proof that God's purposes in judgment are always revelatory: 'they will know that I am the LORD.' Even destruction serves the purpose of divine self-disclosure.
The date formula corresponds to April 29, 587 BC, approximately three months after the oracle in 29:1-16 and shortly after Hophra's failed intervention.

The Hebrew zeroʿa ('arm') is a well-attested metaphor for military strength in Semitic languages. 'It has not been bound up for healing' indicates no bandage or splint was applied—the damage is permanent.

Block analyzes the arm metaphor as an 'anatomy of power' oracle. The body-part symbolism creates a vivid contrast: Pharaoh groans with shattered arms while Nebuchadnezzar wields YHWH's own sword. Block notes that the recognition formula appears twice (vv. 25, 26), once for the Egyptians recognizing YHWH's power over Babylon and once for recognizing YHWH's power in their own scattering.

Zimmerli identifies this as the most historically specific Egypt oracle, directly referencing Hophra's failed campaign. He argues that the arm-breaking metaphor is drawn from royal combat ideology where the pharaoh's arm symbolized his ability to smite enemies. YHWH's breaking of this arm is therefore a direct challenge to Egyptian royal theology.

hist.context: This is the second dated Egypt oracle (April 587 BC, v. 20), arriving after Pharaoh Hophra's brief and unsuccessful attempt to relieve the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem (cf. Jer 37:5-11). Hophra's army advanced but quickly withdrew when Nebuchadnezzar turned to face them. Ezekiel interprets this military failure as YHWH breaking Pharaoh's arm. The remaining arm will also be broken, and the Egyptians will be scattered among nations—the same fate that befell Judah.

themes.note: Judgment peaks as Egypt faces comprehensive destruction—cosmic, geographic, and military. Sovereignty is again paramount as YHWH controls the arms of both Pharaoh and Nebuchadnezzar.

rows.label: vv.1-9
rows.text: Day of the LORD Against Egypt

lit.note: Three originally independent oracles are arranged to present a comprehensive case for Egypt's judgment: cosmic (Day of the LORD), geographic (city catalog), and political (broken arms). Each concludes with the recognition formula.

hebtext.note: The yom YHWH ('Day of the LORD') is one of the most significant eschatological concepts in prophetic literature. Originally associated with Israel's expectation of God fighting for them (cf. Amos 5:18-20), the prophets transformed it into a day of universal judgment. This implies that Egypt's judgment is not arbitrary but occurs at God's determined time, reinforcing the theme of divine sovereignty over historical processes.

The image of Pharaoh dropping his sword with a groan captures total military impotence.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Day of the LORD Against Egypt

translations.text: They will be desolate among desolate lands, and their cities will lie among ruined cities.
And they shall be desolated in the midst of desolated countries, and their cities shall be in the midst of cities that are laid waste.
“Son of man, prophesy and say: ‘This is what the Sovereign Lord says: “ ‘Wail and say, “Alas for that day!”
“Son of man, prophesy, and say, Thus says the Lord GOD: “Wail, ‘Alas for the day!’

thread.anchor: The Day of the LORD Against Egypt
thread.text: Pharaoh's army marched north to relieve Jerusalem but retreated when the Babylonians turned to engage them. This is the 'broken arm' of Ezekiel 30:21—a military failure interpreted as divine judgment.

coaching.tip: 'Wail, for the day is near — the day of the LORD is near' (v. 2–3). The 'Day of the LORD' language, first developed for Israel (Amos 5:18), is now applied to Egypt. God's judgment day isn't exclusive to covenant people — it extends to any nation. The theological category expands as Ezekiel applies it. Every empire eventually has its 'day.'

God breaks Pharaoh's arms — one already broken, the other about to be (v. 21). The 'arms' represent military power. The image is clinical: God as surgeon, methodically disabling the Egyptian war machine. Ezekiel's God doesn't just decree judgment; he executes it with specific, targeted actions. The metaphor of broken arms conveys not random destruction but precise disarmament.
